cue: Ask whether every element of the left set is also in the right.
Ask whether the two sets share no element at all.
note: isdisjoint can stop at the first shared element, so it may beat building the full intersection.

cue: Build the jewel set once, then walk stones by index and count a[i] found in it.
prompt: Write `count_jewels(jewels, stones)` returning how many characters of `stones` appear in the string `jewels`. Each character of `jewels` is distinct.
tests:
assert count_jewels("aA", "aAAbbbb") == 3, count_jewels("aA", "aAAbbbb")
assert count_jewels("z", "ZZ") == 0, count_jewels("z", "ZZ")
assert count_jewels("", "abc") == 0, count_jewels("", "abc")
assert count_jewels("xyz", "") == 0, count_jewels("xyz", "")
note: Set membership keeps this O(len(stones)); scanning the jewels string for each stone would be O(len(jewels)*len(stones)).

cue: Keep the values that land in exactly one of the two sets.
prompt: Write `only_one(a, b)` returning the sorted list of distinct values that appear in exactly one of lists `a` and `b`.
solution:
def only_one(a, b):
    return sorted(set(a) ^ set(b))

cue: Walk lists by index, folding each lists[i] into a growing set, then order the result.
prompt: Write `union_all(lists)` taking a list of lists and returning the sorted list of all distinct values across them.
tests:
assert union_all([[1, 2], [2, 3], [3, 4]]) == [1, 2, 3, 4], union_all([[1, 2], [2, 3], [3, 4]])
assert union_all([]) == [], union_all([])
assert union_all([[]]) == [], union_all([[]])
assert union_all([[5, 5], [5]]) == [5], union_all([[5, 5], [5]])
